food_name: Iced Morel Mushrooms with Soy-Wasabi Dipping Sauce
food_subgroup: Mushrooms and fungi — chilled preparation with condiment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general morel mushroom entry; no specific entry for the chilled/blanched preparation with soy-wasabi dipping sauce. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms and other fungi — fresh or chilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fresh/chilled mushrooms broadly; no specific code for morel (Morchella spp.) chilled preparation. Closest parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific chilled morel mushroom dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Iced Morel Mushrooms is approximately 100 g of blanched and chilled morel mushrooms served atop crushed ice with a small ramekin of soy-wasabi dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, morel, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168422)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw morel mushrooms (FDC ID 168422), adjusted to reflect the blanched preparation (minimal nutrient loss expected for brief blanching) and the addition of a light soy sauce dipping sauce contributing to the elevated sodium figure of 320 mg per 100 g. Core mushroom macros (energy 35 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 0.6 g, carbohydrate 4.5 g, fibre 2.8 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for morels. Sodium elevated from the natural mushroom baseline (~21 mg) to 320 mg to account for soy sauce dipping sauce contribution. Micronutrient values (iron 12 mg, potassium 410 mg, phosphorus 190 mg, vitamin D 5.2 mcg, riboflavin 0.45 mg, niacin 5.0 mg) are consistent with published USDA and peer-reviewed literature values for Morchella spp.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate edible fungi
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Morchella spp. (morel mushrooms) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for low-carbohydrate edible fungi (e.g., oyster mushrooms GI ~15, shiitake mushrooms GI ~10–15) as reported in peer-reviewed literature including Atkinson et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. Morels contain only 4.5 g carbohydrate and 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting a very low GI classification. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (15 × 4.5) / 100 = 0.675, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from soy sauce dipping sauce (320 mg per 100 g); offset partially by high potassium content (410 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish with caution due to the soy sauce dipping sauce contributing approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce or limiting the dipping sauce quantity is advisable to maintain a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g), high dietary fibre (2.8 g per 100 g), and estimated low glycemic index (~15) support stable postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Iced morel mushrooms are well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given their very low carbohydrate load, high fibre content, and estimated low glycemic index of approximately 15, making them unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions; patients should ensure the dipping sauce does not contain added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.6 g per 100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.1 g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium (410 mg) and iron (12 mg) content support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Morel mushrooms are an excellent low-fat, cholesterol-free food for individuals with cardiovascular disease; the high potassium content supports vascular function, though sodium from the soy sauce dipping sauce should be moderated to avoid adverse blood pressure effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Morels are a rare plant-based source of vitamin D (5.2 mcg per 100 g) and contain antioxidant compounds relevant to immune modulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Morel mushrooms may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their vitamin D content (5.2 mcg per 100 g) and antioxidant properties; mushrooms must be blanched as raw morels contain gyromitrin-related toxins that are neutralised by heat.

gout.key_factor: Mushrooms contain purines which may contribute to elevated uric acid levels in susceptible individuals
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia should consume morel mushrooms in moderation as mushrooms contain purines; portion control is recommended and clinical guidance should be sought if uric acid levels are poorly controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Morel Mushrooms with Soy-Wasabi Dipping Sauce (冰镇羊肚菌, Bīngzhèn yángdǔjūn) is a contemporary upscale Chinese appetizer or side dish that showcases the premium quality and distinctive honeycomb texture of morel mushrooms (Morchella spp.). Fresh morel mushrooms are carefully cleaned to remove debris from their characteristic pitted caps, briefly blanched in boiling water to neutralise naturally occurring toxins and preserve their structural integrity, and then immediately plunged into ice water to halt cooking and achieve a firm, crisp texture. The dish is served chilled, typically presented atop a bed of crushed ice to maintain temperature and visual elegance. It is accompanied by a refined dipping sauce composed of light soy sauce with a touch of wasabi or Chinese mustard, providing a sharp, saline, and mildly pungent contrast to the earthy, nutty, and subtly smoky flavour of the morels. Nutritionally, morel mushrooms are exceptionally low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g) and fat (0.6 g per 100 g), with a moderate protein content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low carbohydrates (4.5 g per 100 g). They are notably rich in iron (12 mg per 100 g), potassium (410 mg per 100 g), phosphorus (190 mg per 100 g), riboflavin (0.45 mg per 100 g), niacin (5.0 mg per 100 g), and vitamin D (5.2 mcg per 100 g), making them one of the few plant-based sources of vitamin D. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), making this dish suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical consideration is the sodium content (approximately 320 mg per 100 g) contributed by the soy sauce dipping sauce, which warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, and is prized in modern high-end Chinese cuisine as a health-conscious luxury appetizer.
common_user_queries: Is chilled morel mushroom dish vegan?
What is the glycemic index of morel mushrooms?
Is morel mushroom high in iron?
Contemporary Chinese cold dish with mushrooms

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw morel mushrooms (FDC ID 168422) and peer-reviewed literature on Morchella spp. nutritional composition. The sodium value (320 mg per 100 g) is an adaptation reflecting the soy sauce dipping sauce contribution rather than the mushroom alone. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as no direct GI measurement for morel mushrooms exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Sodium elevated to 320 mg per 100 g from the natural morel mushroom baseline (~21 mg per 100 g per USDA SR Legacy) to account for the soy sauce dipping sauce component of this dish preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for morel mushrooms is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168422) at the level of individual fatty acid classes for this food. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the blanched and chilled preparation of morel mushrooms with dipping sauce is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for morel mushrooms). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for morel mushrooms). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — morel mushrooms are a plant-based food containing no vitamin B12). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — morel mushrooms are a plant-based food containing no cholesterol). 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed literature for low-carbohydrate edible fungi; no direct GI measurement for Morchella spp. exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input JSON (two identical '1 serving (100g)' entries) — deduplicated to single 100 g serving in this output. 7. Input source cited 'Food Research International' which is not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved primary source. 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301502 is the closest available FoodOn entry for morel mushroom food product; no exact entry for the chilled/blanched preparation exists.
